dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a possible cardiac arrest at 923 Evergreen.
Hi, I'm EMT Mike with Rescue Squad 3. What made you call 911 today?
My 18-year-old granddaughter was found unresponsive in the living room.
Let's check her responsiveness. Apply painful stimulus.
No response to voice or touch. No eye opening to verbal stimulus-only responds to pain.
Mimicable movement with no purposeful follow through. Poor response to pain.
She's overdosed before-three times in the past 2-3 weeks.
Airway is patent and self-maintained.
Respiratory rate is 17. Shallow and regular. No cyanosis observed.
Oxygen saturation is 89% in room air. Administering non-rebreather mask at 10 L per minute.
Radial pulse is strong, 97 beats per minute. Skin is normal and dry.
Pulse is 97 and regular.
Respiration is 17 per minute, consistent and shallow.
Blood pressure measured at 100/59 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation at 89% room air, improving on non-rebreather.
EKG shows sinus rhythm. No acute changes noted.
Glucose is not obtainable-results at zero. No signs of hypoglycemia.
Initiating ETCO₂ capnography for real-time airway and perfusion monitoring.
IV line placed in extremity vein on left arm. 18-gauge needle with normal saline lock.
Skin appears dry. No bruising, rashes, or trauma. Good turgor observed.
No abnormalities in head or neck. Pupils small and pinpoint on initial exam.
Capillary refill within 2 seconds. No limb deformity or wound sighted.
She ingested three pills about an hour ago-thought they were "molly".
She has known history of opioid dependence, major depression, and anxiety.
Any other medications or substances around the time of the event?
None that I know of-just those pills.
Any loss of consciousness, vomiting, or fever prior to this incident?
No-was fine until she took those pills.
What was her last meal or anything she consumed?
She last ate several hours ago but nothing after that.
Are you feeling any chest discomfort, nausea, or dizziness?
No pain, just feel very sluggish.
Capnography readings remain normal. IV remains in place on lock.
Using Protocol 3-11 due to suspected opioid intoxication and respiratory concerns.
Now at 95 beats per minute, 10 breaths per minute with more effort.
Blood pressure improved to 127/77 and oxygen saturation is up to 99%.
Repeat EKG continues to show sinus rhythm, no changes.
Are you still feeling sluggish or experiencing pain?
Still feel unwell but not in pain-more alert than before.
Transport destination is St. Mary's Hospital. ER has been notified.
Positioning patient semi-Fowler, restraints on chest, waist, and legs.
IV line secure, no drugs administered by EMS.
No related trauma or bleeding observed throughout the treatment.
First Responders recovery packet reviewed and signed by grandmother.
Patient: 18-year-old female found unresponsive after possible intentional opioid ingestion.
Progressed from AOX0 to AOX4 during initial EMS arrival and treatment.
Oxygen saturation increased from 89% to 99% with NRB. Improved alertness noted.
Pinpoint pupils initially-now at 2-3mm with spontaneous recovery observed.
Previously unresponsive-now oriented and following simple commands.
No additional interventions administered aside from oxygen and IV setup.
Known history of opioid dependence, depression, and anxiety noted.
No access to ID or insurance at time of care-MRN utilized for hospital registration.
ER notified of patient's known issues and delays anticipated due to bed availability.
Transported using Reeves stretcher with complete restraint for patient safety.
Patient prior unresponsive, now alert and conscious during handoff.
Spontaneous awakening occurred following EMS arrival-no antidote given.
No trauma, JVD, or pedal edema observed during transport or immobilization.
Capnography and IV established. EKG monitoring applied during care.
Denied any recent illness. Increased awareness during transport upon regaining consciousness.
ER admitted patient without interference, despite noted delays due to bed availability.
Patient signed for release document-fully aware and oriented during process.
No additional medical interventions performed. Current status: stable.
Mobile phone documentation updated with GPS alerts for care coordination.
No signs of distress or aggressive behavior observed. EMS recommended ongoing protocols.
Patients current status classified for stable transfer to ER.
Documented follow guidelines regarding overdose patient case management.
No transport-related errors noted during vehicle exit to hospital bed staging.
Patient fully documented post-handover with relevant timestamps and vital signs.
No instability following transfer-semi-Fowler's position maintained for optimal mobility.
All documentation forms completed, ready for hospital intake verification.
No delay or restriction in movement-patient able to confirm handover procedures.
Updates set to roll out during intake for timely medical decisions.
Patient indoor documentation digitally synced for receiving care team.
Hospital facility accepts patient without prior call on admission sign-off.
All critical capacity readiness metrics secured for initiating proper care.
No delays expected from transportation handling to ER check-ins.
Concluded intake operations using first aid major principles without issues.
Patient reporting performance verified before sign-up to anonymous record.
Patient MRN captured for navigating admission registration procedures.
No evidence of operational ethics during entire transfer process.
Turns okay for pathway control for bed reassignment.
No red flag for non-degree threats on temporal basis.
No requirement for critical internal emergency until current bed is free.
Handover position standardized for patient route to hospital safekeeping.
No directly possible future complications witnessed per hospital routines.
Next monitoring stage prepped before final effort to transfer.
No breathing clip or motion disapproval deemed necessary at time.
Patient selected destination confirmed based on hospital responses.
Patient's care plan was declared as per best protocols for known overdose symptoms.
Empathy supported but medical attention tracked likewise for future subjects.
Consistent medical advice documented for update by known caregiver.
No personal surveillance required for current engagements as per protocol.
Hospital signages exact as required for process declarations when needed.
Allowed trends specific to semi-hospital movements during transit.
No documentation problems during bed resource prompts-no medication trails provided.
Health verification aligned through family resources for leading patient first name identity.
No bottlenecks in mass implementation of patient accommodations.
No emergency threshold reached, no behavioral descriptors recorded in data.
Defined time matched with hospital's ambient control track.
Final instruction expectation maintained in case more assistance is required.
